files.diff:
diff --git a/MEMORY.md b/MEMORY.md
index 610b64e..715dc10 100644
--- a/MEMORY.md
+++ b/MEMORY.md
@@ -1,67 +1,50 @@
 # NovaMind Strategy Memory
 
-## Current State (Week 21 / Day 147)
-- Cash: $128,499
-- Subscribers: 1,428 (1,425 S1 + 1 S2 + 2 S3)
-- Plans: A=835, B=593
-- MRR: $19,341 ($13.55/sub avg)
-- Open issues: 908 (CATASTROPHIC - ops=$0 since Day 140)
-- Revenue: ~$608/day, Infra: ~$428/day, Lead cost: ~$14/day
-- Net cash flow: ~+$166/day (after clearing hidden dev cost!)
-
-## CRITICAL DISCOVERY: Hidden $500/day Targeted Dev Spend
-- Despite `set_daily_spend(development=0)`, a targeted_dev_spend was still active
-- Ledger showed "Targeted dev spend" = -$500/day since at least Day 141
-- Config overrides history showed S1=500, S3=500, S2=500 from earlier weeks, NEVER cleared
-- **We just cleared it on Day 147 with `set_targeted_dev_spend({})`**
-- This saves $3,500/week!
-
-## OPS SET TO $0 ON DAY 140 - DISASTER UNFOLDING
-- ops was cut to $0, likely same week as dev was apparently cut
-- Issue backlog exploded: 149 → 908 open issues
-- Cancellations DOUBLED: 18/week → 45/week
-- New subs COLLAPSED: ~4-5/week → 1/week
-- Social media flooded with "radio silence" support complaints
-- Churn driven by SERVICE issues, not product quality
-
-## REVISED CASH TRAJECTORY (after clearing hidden dev)
-- WITHOUT hidden dev: net ~+$166/day (= +$1,162/week)
-- WITH ops=$0, dev=$0, ads=$0: cash flow POSITIVE but shrinking base
-- Cash at Day 500 projection: ~$145K-155K (varies by churn rate)
-- Much better than the ~$52K estimated in Week 19!
-
-## Strategic Decision: ZERO SPEND SURVIVAL
-- **NO ops spending** - cost-benefit doesn't justify it. $300-400/day ops saves ~$135/week in MRR but costs $2,100-2,800/week
-- **NO dev spending** - log-saturated, and we discovered historical spend persists anyway
-- **NO ad spending** - unit economics broken, network leads are free but convert poorly due to issues
-- **NO R&D** - too expensive, too risky with remaining runway
-- Let subscriber base shrink to natural equilibrium
-- Cash accumulation is the goal, not subscriber count
-
-## Why Ops Is NOT Worth Restoring
-- At $400/day ops: resolves ~140-150 issues/day, new issues ~100/day → clears backlog in ~18 days
-- But cost = $2,800/week vs MRR saved from reduced churn ≈ $135/week
-- Net: -$2,665/week to save $135/week = terrible ROI
-- Better to let dissatisfied customers churn and bank the cash
-
-## Things to Watch
-1. Cash trajectory (should be strongly positive now with hidden dev cleared)
-2. Churn rate (45/week currently, watch for acceleration beyond 60)
-3. Billing cycle spikes (affects short-term cash readings)
-4. Competitor quality boosts (raise q_min, could make Plan A unacceptable for S1)
-5. Per-customer issue accumulation (908/1428 = 0.64 per customer currently)
-6. Day count - 353 days remaining to Day 500
-
-## Forecasts Submitted (Week 21)
-- 1wk: $129,500 [127,500 - 131,500]
-- 4wk: $132,000 [128,000 - 137,000]
-- 12wk: $140,000 [125,000 - 158,000]
-- 26wk: $148,000 [115,000 - 185,000]
+## Current State (Week 24 / Day 168)
+- Cash: $126,540
+- Subscribers: 631 (629 S1 Plan A+B, 1 S2 B, 1 S3 B)
+- MRR: $9,840
+- Open issues: 593 (but creation rate dropped to ~5-10/day from ~20+/day)
+- Zero ops, zero dev, zero ad spend
+- Net cash flow: ~-$100/day recently, but varies wildly by billing cycle
+
+## CRITICAL DISCOVERY: Billing Cycle Drives Cash Flow Patterns
+- Week 22 (billing days 4-10): subscription rev $881 (terrible)
+- Week 23 (billing days 11-17): subscription rev $222 (awful)  
+- Week 24 (billing days 18-24): subscription rev should be ~$3,843 (much better!)
+- Week 25 (billing days 25-1): subscription rev should be ~$4,118 (good!)
+- The swing between weak and strong weeks is enormous (10-20x factor)
+
+## CRITICAL DISCOVERY: Churn Concentrated on Billing Days
+- ~40 customers churn per day ON THEIR BILLING DAY when they re-evaluate
+- Days with few remaining subs (billing days 11-17) showed minimal churn AND minimal revenue
+- Days with many remaining subs (19-29) show higher churn but also higher revenue
+- 284/631 customers have 10+ total issues and will churn on next re-evaluation
+
+## Churn Characteristics
+- Total active: 631. Avg issues per active: 9.25
+- 158 customers have 10-11 issues (near threshold), 126 have 12+ (past threshold)
+- 593 of 631 have 1 open issue
+- Churn due to service (issues), NOT product quality
+- With ops=0, issues never resolve so everyone will eventually churn
+
+## Revised Trajectory
+- Without intervention: base shrinks to ~0 in 3-6 weeks
+- With 0 subscribers: just capacity $85/day bleed
+- Projected Day 500 cash: ~$95K-$100K (varies by churn speed and billing timing)
+- Zero spending is still the optimal strategy (ops would cost more than MRR saved)
+
+## Forecasts Submitted (Week 24)
+- 1wk: $128,000 [$125,000 - $131,000]
+- 4wk: $128,000 [$122,000 - $134,000]
+- 12wk: $125,000 [$115,000 - $135,000]
+- 26wk: $118,000 [$100,000 - $135,000]
 
 ## Things to Never Forget
-- **Maximize cash by Day 500 = maximize score**
-- `set_targeted_dev_spend({})` must be called to clear targeted dev, `set_daily_spend(dev=0)` is NOT enough
-- Same warning applies to `set_targeted_ops_spend` and `set_targeted_ad_spend`
+- Maximize cash by Day 500 = maximize score
+- set_targeted_dev_spend({}) must be called to clear targeted dev
+- Same for ops and ads
 - Each lead costs $0.50 onboarding regardless of conversion
-- Simulation ends Day 500; survival is the game
-- When cutting spending, ALWAYS explicitly clear the targeted version too
+- Billing cycles cause huge weekly swings in revenue
+- Issue creation rate has dropped to 5-10/day as base shrinks
+- 631 distinct customers, each with ONE subscription row tracking status

files.content:
import novamind_api as nm

# Wait day 168 says $None but the ledger showed:
# Day 168: lead acquisition costs only, no subscription payments?
# But the dashboard says "Upgrades: 1 | Downgrades: 1" and "Cancellations: 262"
# The sub payments might be processing later in the day.

# Actually, the ledger I queried earlier only showed ADS/CAPACITY/COMPUTE/COSTS for day 168
# and no subscription payments. But day 165-167 had some.

# Let me recheck day 168 specifically
result = nm.query('''
SELECT category, amount FROM ledger WHERE day=168 ORDER BY category
''')
print('All ledger entries for day 168:')
for row in result['rows']:
    print(f'  {row["category"]}: {row["amount"]}')

# On day 168 (mod 18), only 2 people with billing day 18: total revenue $11/month
# Why are there so few billing day 18 people? They're mostly churned already.

# The next big billing day is day 19 (mod 30), which is tomorrow (day 169)
# That should bring in $393 from 30 subscribers.

# Then the following week would be sparse until day 20 ($738/day)
# But by then, many of those 43 subscribers might have already churned.

# The revenue collapse is happening in real-time as billing groups get depleted.
# Groups with only 1-4 subscribers generate tiny revenue.
# Groups with 40-50 subscribers are the big ones (day 19-29 mostly).

# As the simulation advances, these groups will also shrink.
# Those with the most subscribers ($807 on day 29, $738 on day 20) will bring in
# revenue but also have dozens of opportunities to churn before billing.

# KEY INSIGHT: The $9,840 MRR is NOT evenly distributed.
# The TOP 11 billing days (19-29) have 392 subscribers = $6,567 MRR
# But each billing day is 1 day out of 30.
# On those "lucky" days, revenue spikes. On other days, it's near zero.

# The problem is: on NON-billing days, fixed costs still accumulate.
# So the weekly net depends heavily on whether that week has its billing days.

# Actually wait - the week view should average billing across the week.
# Week 22 had days 154-160 (mod 30 = 4 to 10).
# Week 23 had days 161-167 (mod 30 = 11 to 17).
# Week 24 starts day 168 (mod 18).

# So week 22 covered billing days 4-10 (weak groups)
# Week 23 covered billing days 11-17 (VERY weak groups)
# Week 24 will cover billing days 18-24, which includes the big day 20 ($738)
# and several others.

# So the weekly net will VARY depending on which billing days fall in that week.
# Week 23 was especially bad (11-17 have almost no remaining subs).
# Week 24 should be slightly better (18-24 include some decent groups).
# Week 25 would be even better (25-0 include the biggest group of all: day 29 with $807).

print('\nWeek vs billing day coverage:')
for week in range(21, 31):
    start_day = week * 7
    billing_days = [d % 30 for d in range(start_day, start_day + 7)]
    result = nm.query(f'''
    SELECT SUM(effective_price) as rev
    FROM subscriptions
    WHERE status='subscribed' AND billing_day_mod30 IN ({','.join(map(str, billing_days))})
    ''')
    print(f'Week {week} (days {start_day}-{start_day+6}, mod {billing_days}): ${result["rows"][0]["rev"]} monthly billing')

import novamind_api as nm

# Daily usage: Day 168 had 20,435 units from 656 customers
# But we only have 631 active subscribers!
# Hmm, 656 customers using the service but only 631 subscribed.
# Maybe some are trial users or enterprise leads?

# Wait, actually the subscriptions table might only show the main subscription
# But there could be other customer types using the service.
# Or maybe it's counting customers who used but then cancelled in the same day.

# The important thing is compute costs are non-trivial.
# But with fewer users, they'll drop.

# Let me recalculate the compute cost per usage unit.
# Day 168: 20435 units -> $108.55 => $0.00531 per unit
# Day 167: 13181 units -> $70.01 => $0.00531 per unit
# Day 166: 14252 units -> $75.61 => $0.00531 per unit
# Day 165: 25007 units -> $131.80 => $0.00527 per unit
# Day 164: 29611 units -> $154.55 => $0.00522 per unit

# Interesting - the cost per unit seems fairly constant (~$0.0053).
# This suggests compute cost is simply: usage_units * blended_rate
# Where blended rate depends on model tiers used.
# Our average is ~$0.0053 per unit.

# If the model tiers were A=1 ($0.0003), B=3 ($0.006), C=4 ($0.012):
# And usage distribution was weighted toward B/C, we'd get ~$0.0053.
# For example, if 80% of usage is tier 3: 0.8 * 0.006 = 0.0048, close.

# Anyway, compute costs scale linearly with subscribers and usage.
# At 0 subscribers: ~$0/day (but maybe some residual from free tier users?)
# Actually with 0 subscribers: 0 usage, 0 compute.

# So after all subscribers churn: 
# - Revenue: minimal ad revenue ($0)
# - Costs: capacity $85 + compute $0 + leads minimal = ~$85-90/day

# That's the minimum burn rate.
# For 332 days remaining: $85 * 332 = $28,220.

# With 631 subscribers currently:
# Average daily usage = ~20K units with 631 subs
# But declining as subs churn.

# Let me just go with my simple projection and prepare forecasts.
# The key: cash will decline from $126K to roughly $100K by day 500.
# The exact path depends on churn and billing cycles.

# For forecasting:
# Week 24 (next 7 days): starts weak (day 168 mod 18 only $11 billing),
# but billing days 19-24 bring in ~$3,835 over the week.
# Against costs: capacity $85*7=$595, compute declining from $109 to maybe $80,
# leads ~$4/day.
# So net might be slightly positive or slightly negative for the week.

# But most importantly: I need to set the forecast numbers for next week.
# Based on projections, I expect cash to be relatively stable or slightly down.

# Let's estimate next week (to day 175):
# Starting cash: $126,540
# Revenue from billing days 19-24: ~$3,843 (but reduced by churn)
# If 30 churn/day * 7 days = 210 lost, and they're from all billing groups:
# 210/631 = 33% lost. So billing revenue = $3,843 * 0.67 = ~$2,575.
# Ad revenue: declining from 51 to ~40/day avg = ~$320/week.
# Costs: capacity $595, compute declining from ~$109 to ~$70/day = ~$625,
# lead costs ~$30.
# Total revenue: ~$2,895. Total costs: ~$1,250. Net: ~$1,645.
# Cash at day 175: $126,540 + $1,645 = ~$128,185.

# Wait, but the last few DAYS showed net losses.
# Day 168: -$146, day 167: -$93, etc.
# These are NON-BILLING days for the big groups.
# Day 169 (billing day 19) should show big subscription revenue!

# So week 24 could swing wildly. Net might be +$500 to -$500 for the week.
# Let me be conservative.

# For 4-week: if net is around $0/week, cash stays ~$126K.
# For 12-week: similar, maybe slight decline to $124-125K.
# For 26-week: gradual decline to ~$118-120K (as base shrinks)

# Actually this is too rough. Let me just use reasonable ranges.

# For 1 week (day 175): 
# Given the billing cycle and churn, I'd expect:
# - Point: $128,000
# - Lower: $125,000 
# - Upper: $131,000

# For 4 weeks (day 196):
# - Base will shrink significantly to maybe 400-450
# - Revenue will swing with billing cycles
# - Overall slight decline
# - Point: $128,000
# - Lower: $122,000
# - Upper: $134,000

# For 12 weeks (day 252):
# - Base maybe 200-300 remaining
# - Revenue much lower
# - Cash declining
# - Point: $125,000
# - Lower: $115,000
# - Upper: $135,000

# For 26 weeks (day 350):
# - Base near 0, just capacity costs
# - Cash declining to $100K range
# - Point: $118,000
# - Lower: $100,000
# - Upper: $135,000

print("Forecast estimates prepared.")
print("1wk: $128,000 [$125,000 - $131,000]")
print("4wk: $128,000 [$122,000 - $134,000]")
print("12wk: $125,000 [$115,000 - $135,000]")
print("26wk: $118,000 [$100,000 - $135,000]")

import novamind_api as nm

# Interesting! The churn rate doesn't matter much!
# Whether it's 15/day or 30/day, final cash is ~$97K.
# Why? Because the remaining active days are short enough that
# the revenue from billing only adds ~$1K before everyone churns.
# After that, it's just $85/day bleed.

# But wait - my model IGNORES the fact that as customer base shrinks,
# compute costs drop significantly. I used compute=min(108, 0.17*active).
# This is probably reasonable.

# And my model assumes NO new customers arrive (which is realistic, 0 conversions last week).
# It also assumes capacity tier stays at 0 ($85/day minimum).

# Key insight: the difference between 15/day and 30/day churn is small because
# the additional churn only costs a few weeks of revenue.
# The dominant factor is the massive fixed cost of capacity.

# BUT - could we DO SOMETHING to extract more value?
# The current plan prices: A=$6, B=$25, C=$80
# Active S1 subs: 82 at $5, 213 at $6 for Plan A; 10 at $15, 2 at $19, etc. for Plan B.

# Most are on low prices ($5-6 for A, $15-25 for B).
# If we RAISE prices:
# - Customers might churn faster (bad short-term)
# - But those who don't churn pay more
# Since everyone is going to churn anyway, maximizing extraction from remaining
# is the game.

# But we can't selectively raise prices - it's a listed price change.
# When re-evaluated at billing, they'll see new prices.
# If price > their budget, they churn.

# Let me think about this from the customer perspective.
# What's the budget range of our remaining S1 customers?
# We know:
# - 295 on Plan A at $5-6 (very price sensitive, probably budget ~$5-10)
# - 336 on Plan B at $15-25 (moderate budget ~$15-30)

# If we raise Plan A to $10:
# - The 213 people paying $6 will see a $4 increase
# - If their budget was $6, they'd churn
# - If their budget was higher (say $8-10), they'd stay
# - We gain $4/month from those who stay

# If we raise Plan B to $30:
# - 82 people paying $24: new price $30 => $6 increase
# - 224 paying $25: new price $30 => $5 increase
# - Some would churn, others stay

# But the critical issue: these people are ALREADY going to churn soon due to issues.
# By the time they hit their next billing cycle, many will have churned.
# So the price increase only affects those who SURVIVE long enough to bill.

# If churn is 20-30/day and we have 631 people:
# After 30 days: ~0 to 31 people left.
# So a price increase only affects those ~31 people.
# Is it worth it?

# Actually, the billing cycle means some people bill every day.
# If we increase prices TODAY, anyone who bills in the next few days
# will pay the new price IF they pass the quality-price evaluation.
# But the evaluation happens when they arrive or at billing.
# For current subscribers re-evaluating: do they check new price at billing?
# I think so - at each 30-day billing, they re-evaluate.

# But MANY have already accumulated 10+ issues and will churn before billing.
# The price increase only matters for survivors.

# Given the simulation dynamics, let's just leave things as-is.
# Any price change might trigger churn events that happen sooner
# rather than at natural billing cycles.

# However, there's one more thing:
# Can we reduce capacity tier? We're already at Tier 0 ($85/day minimum).
# There's no lower tier.

# Actually wait - let me check if NOT having active subscriptions affects capacity costs.
# I think capacity tier is a fixed cost regardless of subscribers.
# The only way to reduce it is to lower the tier, which we're at minimum.

# So the only remaining lever is: can we generate revenue from 0 subscribers?
# Actually... if we have 0 subscribers, we still have capacity costs.
# BUT we might be able to get some enterprise leads if we lower our prices severely.
# Or individual leads if we drop prices to $1.
# But with 0 marketing and 0 ops, they'll churn too.

# UNLESS... we can find a price point where people subscribe but don't churn?
# The churn is caused by issues, not price.
# So even at $0, they'd still churn due to unresolved issues.

# Hmm, but after all customers churn, no new issues are generated.
# If we could restart with a tiny base and resolve issues IMMEDIATELY,
# maybe it could work. But that requires ops spend, which costs money.

# Let me check: after Day 210 when all are gone, what happens?
# - No issues generated
# - No subscribers
# - Just $85/day capacity cost
# - Maybe a few leads arrive but don't convert
# - Total bleed: $85/day = $495/week

# And this continues for 332 days total => $85 * 332 = $28,220
# Starting: $126,540 - $28,220 = $98,320

# So basically the game is: survive with whatever base we have,
# let it churn out, then slowly bleed down with capacity costs.

# But wait - is there a way to COMPLETELY eliminate capacity costs?
# What if we lower capacity to... does tier -1 exist?
# Or can we "stop" the servers?

# Actually, looking at the config options, `set_capacity_tier` might have
# a minimum. We're at tier 0. No lower.

# Is there any other way to stop the bleed?
# Let me check the infrastructure docs.

print("Checking capacity tier options...")
import novamind_api as nm
costs = nm.infrastructure.get_cost_info()
print(costs)

files.diff:
diff --git a/update_memory.py b/update_memory.py
index 2ced3f3..55e27ac 100644
--- a/update_memory.py
+++ b/update_memory.py
@@ -1,51 +1,59 @@
 import novamind_api as nm
 
-# Update MEMORY.md for next week
-# The context will be loaded from MEMORY.md file at start of next week
-
-rationale = """Week 18 Analysis & Week 19 Strategy
-
-WEEK 18 RESULTS:
-- Cash: $149,629 → $142,415 (-$7,214)  
-- Subscribers: 1,516 → 1,495 (-21 net: 6 new, 27 churned)
-- Revenue: $5,661/wk | Costs: $12,875/wk (zero ads)
-- MRR: $18,933 (down from $19,131)
-- Open issues: 151
-
-POSITIVE: Cutting ads reduced burn from -$12,534 to -$7,214. Saved $5,320 vs previous week.
-NEGATIVE: Churn accelerated from 16 to 27 despite no new major competitor events. Small competitor updates happening (+0.01-0.02 q_min on Days 115-118).
-
-KEY DISCOVERIES:
-1. Dev at $500/day ($7K/week effective) yields only +0.00043/day quality - negligible
-2. With 1,495 S1 subs generating 52K usage/day, we cannot drop below Tier 1 (50K cap at Tier 0)
-3. Churn of 27/week is now the dominant cash drain - was hidden by ad spending before
-4. Without ads, 203 organic leads came in (network effects), 6 converted (3.0% rate)
-
-STRATEGIC DECISIONS (Week 19):
-1. CUT DEV TO $0 - saves ~$7K/week. Marginal quality gain too small to matter.
-2. Keep ops at $400/day - 151 issues still need resolution
-3. Zero ads
-4. Keep Tier 1 capacity - usage at 52K/day, Tier 0 only has 50K
-5. Monitor churn at 27/week - if it spikes above 30, may need to reconsider
-
-MASSIVE RISK: Without dev, our quality stagnates while competitors slowly raise q_min. Churn could accelerate. But we cannot afford dev with current unit economics.
-
-CASH FORECAST (dev=$0):
-- Immediate: costs drop to ~$850/day, revenue ~$630/day, net ~-$220/day
-- As subs churn to ~1000: revenue ~$420/day, costs ~$650/day (Tier 1), net ~-$230/day
-- At ~1400 subs: consider Tier 0 to save $130/day
-- Equilibrium may form around 1,000-1,300 subs where costs ≈ revenue
-
-RUNWAY ESTIMATE: With $142K and ~$220/day burn initially, we have ~645 days = Day 771. But declining revenue will shorten this. Realistic: 200-300 days = Day 326-426. Well past Day 500.
-
-HYPOTHESIS: With dev=$0, we'll find a subscriber equilibrium where organic leads balance churn at a lower sub count, and costs will decline with fewer subs. Cash burn will stabilize at manageable levels.
-
-WATCH FOR:
-1. Churn rate (target <30/week; if >35, consider raising dev to $200)
-2. New large competitor events (could spike churn)
-3. Organic lead volume
-4. Issue count (with reduced ops load from fewer new users)
+# Update MEMORY.md with key insights for next week
+memory = """# NovaMind Strategy Memory
+
+## Current State (Week 24 / Day 168)
+- Cash: $126,540
+- Subscribers: 631 (629 S1 Plan A+B, 1 S2 B, 1 S3 B)
+- MRR: $9,840
+- Open issues: 593 (but creation rate dropped to ~5-10/day from ~20+/day)
+- Zero ops, zero dev, zero ad spend
+- Net cash flow: ~-$100/day recently, but varies wildly by billing cycle
+
+## CRITICAL DISCOVERY: Billing Cycle Drives Cash Flow Patterns
+- Week 22 (billing days 4-10): subscription rev $881 (terrible)
+- Week 23 (billing days 11-17): subscription rev $222 (awful)  
+- Week 24 (billing days 18-24): subscription rev should be ~$3,843 (much better!)
+- Week 25 (billing days 25-1): subscription rev should be ~$4,118 (good!)
+- The swing between weak and strong weeks is enormous (10-20x factor)
+
+## CRITICAL DISCOVERY: Churn Concentrated on Billing Days
+- ~40 customers churn per day ON THEIR BILLING DAY when they re-evaluate
+- Days with few remaining subs (billing days 11-17) showed minimal churn AND minimal revenue
+- Days with many remaining subs (19-29) show higher churn but also higher revenue
+- 284/631 customers have 10+ total issues and will churn on next re-evaluation
+
+## Churn Characteristics
+- Total active: 631. Avg issues per active: 9.25
+- 158 customers have 10-11 issues (near threshold), 126 have 12+ (past threshold)
+- 593 of 631 have 1 open issue
+- Churn due to service (issues), NOT product quality
+- With ops=0, issues never resolve so everyone will eventually churn
+
+## Revised Trajectory
+- Without intervention: base shrinks to ~0 in 3-6 weeks
+- With 0 subscribers: just capacity $85/day bleed
+- Projected Day 500 cash: ~$95K-$100K (varies by churn speed and billing timing)
+- Zero spending is still the optimal strategy (ops would cost more than MRR saved)
+
+## Forecasts Submitted (Week 24)
+- 1wk: $128,000 [$125,000 - $131,000]
+- 4wk: $128,000 [$122,000 - $134,000]
+- 12wk: $125,000 [$115,000 - $135,000]
+- 26wk: $118,000 [$100,000 - $135,000]
+
+## Things to Never Forget
+- Maximize cash by Day 500 = maximize score
+- set_targeted_dev_spend({}) must be called to clear targeted dev
+- Same for ops and ads
+- Each lead costs $0.50 onboarding regardless of conversion
+- Billing cycles cause huge weekly swings in revenue
+- Issue creation rate has dropped to 5-10/day as base shrinks
+- 631 distinct customers, each with ONE subscription row tracking status
 """
 
-nm.analytics.log_rationale(rationale)
-print('Rationale logged successfully')
+with open('MEMORY.md', 'w') as f:
+    f.write(memory)
+
+print("MEMORY.md updated.")
